subject: Physics
q: For LED's to emit light in visible region of electromagnetic light, it should have energy band gap in the range of:
question: For LED's to emit light in visible region of electromagnetic light, it should have energy band gap in the range of:
solution:
Energy band gap range is given by,
$$
\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}
$$

For visible region
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\lambda=\left(4 \times 10^{-7} \sim 7 \times 10^{-7}\right) \mathrm{m} \\
&\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{7 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&=\frac{19.8 \times 10^{-26}}{7 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&=\frac{2.8 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\
&\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{g}}=1.75 \mathrm{eV}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The maximum velocity of the photoelectrons emitted from the surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> when light of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> falls on a metal surface. If the incident frequency is increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>n</mi></math>, the maximum velocity of the ejected photoelectrons will be:
question: The maximum velocity of the photoelectrons emitted from the surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> when light of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> falls on a metal surface. If the incident frequency is increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>n</mi></math>, the maximum velocity of the ejected photoelectrons will be:
solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric effect, 

KEmax=hcλ-ϕ

The incident energy of the photon is E1=12mv2=hn-ϕ  ...1

If incident frequency increased to 3n

E2=12mv22=3hn- ϕ

=3hn-ϕ+2ϕ 

E2=3 E1+2ϕ

⇒12mv22=32mv2+2ϕ

⇒v22=3v2+4ϕm

⇒v2>v3

subject: Physics
q: An electron in the ground state of the hydrogen atom has the orbital radius of $5.3 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}$ while that for the electron in third excited state is $8.48 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$. The ratio of the de Broglie wavelengths of electron in the excited state to that in the ground state is
question: An electron in the ground state of the hydrogen atom has the orbital radius of $5.3 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}$ while that for the electron in third excited state is $8.48 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$. The ratio of the de Broglie wavelengths of electron in the excited state to that in the ground state is
solution:
$\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi} \\
& \mathrm{mv}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}} \\
& \lambda=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{rh}}{\mathrm{nh}} \\
& \lambda \propto \frac{\mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{n}} \\
& \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_4}=\frac{\mathrm{r}_1 \mathrm{n}_4}{\mathrm{n}_1 \mathrm{r}_4}=\frac{5.3 \times 10^{-11} \times 4}{1 \times 84.8 \times 10^{-11}} \\
& \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_4}=\frac{1}{4}
\end{aligned}$

q: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle and a proton are accelerated from rest by a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. After this, their de Broglie wavelengths are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> respectively. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is :
question: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle and a proton are accelerated from rest by a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. After this, their de Broglie wavelengths are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> respectively. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is :
solution:
λ=hp=h2mqV

⇒λpλα=mαqαmpqp=4mp×2emp×e=8=22=22

⇒λpλα=2×1.4=2.8

subject: Physics
q: When photon of energy <math><mn>4.0</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> strikes the surface of a metal <math><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the ejected photoelectrons have maximum kinetic energy <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and de-Broglie wavelength <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons liberated from another metal <math><mi>B</mi></math> by photon of energy <math><mn>4.50</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>1.5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the de-Broglie wavelength of these photoelectrons <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> then the work function of metal <math><mi>B</mi></math> is:
question: When photon of energy <math><mn>4.0</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> strikes the surface of a metal <math><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the ejected photoelectrons have maximum kinetic energy <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and de-Broglie wavelength <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons liberated from another metal <math><mi>B</mi></math> by photon of energy <math><mn>4.50</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>1.5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the de-Broglie wavelength of these photoelectrons <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> then the work function of metal <math><mi>B</mi></math> is:
solution:
Relation between de-Broglie wavelength and K.E. is
λ=h2(KE)me⇒λ∝1KE
λAλB=KEBKEA
⇒12=TA-1.5TA⇒TA=2 eV
∴KEB=2-1.5=0.5 eV
ϕB=4.5-0.5=4 eV

subject: Physics
q: The stopping potential for photoelectrons emitted from a surface illuminated by light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6630</mn><mo>Å</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>42</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. If the threshold frequency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>13</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is (nearest integer): (Given, speed light <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.  Planck's constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>63</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>)
question: The stopping potential for photoelectrons emitted from a surface illuminated by light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6630</mn><mo>Å</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>42</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. If the threshold frequency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>13</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is (nearest integer): (Given, speed light <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.  Planck's constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>63</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>)
solution:
We know that, KEmax=hf-ϕ=hcλ-ϕ

Or, hcλ-ϕ=eV0, where, ϕ=hνth and V0 is stopping potential.

So, threshold frequency is 

νth=cλ-eV0h

=3×10866330×10-10-1.6×10-19×0.426.63×10-34

=36.630×1015-1.6×0.426.63×1015

=101536.630-1.6×0.426.63=0.4524-0.1013

νth=35.11×1013

subject: Physics
q: A proton and an electron have the same de Broglie wavelength. If $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{e}}$ be the kinetic energies of proton and electron respectively, then choose the correct relation :
question: A proton and an electron have the same de Broglie wavelength. If $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{e}}$ be the kinetic energies of proton and electron respectively, then choose the correct relation :
solution:
De Broglie wavelength of proton \& electron $=\lambda$
$\begin{aligned} & \because \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{p}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{p}_{\text {proton }}=\mathrm{p}_{\text {electron }} \\ & \because \mathrm{KE}=\frac{\mathrm{p}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{KE}_{\text {proton }} < \mathrm{KE}_{\text {electron }} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}} < \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}\right]}\end{aligned}$

question: Given figure shows few data points in a photo-electric effect experiment for a certain metal. The minimum energy for ejection of electrons from its surface is: (Planck's constant h=6.62×10–34 J.s)
solution:
Threshold Energy =hv

=6.62×10−34×5.5×1014 J

Work friction =6.62×5.5×10−201.6×10−19eV

=2.27 eV

subject: Physics
q:
With reference to the observations in photo-electric effect, identify the correct statements from below:

A. The square of maximum velocity of photoelectrons varies linearly with frequency of incident light.

B. The value of saturation current increases on moving the source of light away from the metal surface.

C. The maximum kinetic energy of photo-electrons decreases on decreasing the power of LED (light emitting diode) source of light.

D. The immediate emission of photo-electrons out of metal surface can not be explained by particle nature of light/electromagnetic waves.
E. Existence of threshold wavelength can not be explained by wave nature of light/electromagnetic waves.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
With reference to the observations in photo-electric effect, identify the correct statements from below:

A. The square of maximum velocity of photoelectrons varies linearly with frequency of incident light.

B. The value of saturation current increases on moving the source of light away from the metal surface.

C. The maximum kinetic energy of photo-electrons decreases on decreasing the power of LED (light emitting diode) source of light.

D. The immediate emission of photo-electrons out of metal surface can not be explained by particle nature of light/electromagnetic waves.
E. Existence of threshold wavelength can not be explained by wave nature of light/electromagnetic waves.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, 

Maximum kinetic energy, KEmax=12mvmax2=hf-ϕ, where, vmax is maximum velocity of photoelectrons, h is Planck's constant, f is frequency of incident light and ϕ is work function of metal.

From above relation, we can see that the square of maximum velocity of photoelectrons varies linearly with frequency of incident light.

If we increase the intensity of the incident radiation by keeping the frequency constant, then the value of saturation current increases in proportion to the intensity of the incident radiation. Thus, saturation current decreases on moving the source of light away from the metal surface.

The kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is directly proportional to the light frequency, and it remains constant as light amplitude or power of source increases.

Photoelectric effect cannot be explained on the basis of wave nature because experimentally a particular energy called the work function of the metal surface in needed to eject electrons from surface, irrespective of the time for which light is incident. 

Photoelectric effect can be explained by particle nature of light (i.e. quantum theory). Threshold wavelength is maximum wavelength at which emission takes place.

subject: Physics
q:
The electric field of light wave is given as  E →=10–3cos⁡2πx5×10–7-2π×6×1014 tx^NC . This light falls on a metal plate of work function 2 eV . The stopping potential of the photo-electrons is:
Given, E (in eV ) =12375λ(in Å)
question:
The electric field of light wave is given as  E →=10–3cos⁡2πx5×10–7-2π×6×1014 tx^NC . This light falls on a metal plate of work function 2 eV . The stopping potential of the photo-electrons is:
Given, E (in eV ) =12375λ(in Å)
solution:
E→=10-3cos2πx5×10-7-2π×6×1014tx^ N/c
k=2π5×10-7
And ω=6×1014×2π ⇒f=6×1014 Hz
E=ϕ+KEmax
KEmax=hf-ϕ
=6.6×10-34×6×10141.6×10-19-2
=2.475-2=0.48 eV
∴ Stopping potential is 0.48 V

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion $\mathbf{A}$ and the other is labelled as Reason R.
Assertion A: Number of photons increases with increase in frequency of light.
Reason R: Maximum kinetic energy of emitted electrons increases with the frequency of incident radiation.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion $\mathbf{A}$ and the other is labelled as Reason R.
Assertion A: Number of photons increases with increase in frequency of light.
Reason R: Maximum kinetic energy of emitted electrons increases with the frequency of incident radiation.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are correct and $\mathbf{R}$ is the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$.
Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are correct and $\mathbf{R}$ is NOT the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$.
$\mathbf{A}$ is not correct but $\mathbf{R}$ is correct.
$\mathbf{A}$ is correct but $\mathbf{R}$ is not correct.
solution:
Intensity of light $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{nh} \nu}{\mathrm{A}}$ Here $\mathrm{n}$ is no. of photons per unit time. $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{IA}}{\mathrm{h} \nu}$ so on increasing frequency $v, \mathrm{n}$ decreases taking intensity constant. $\mathrm{k}_{\max }=\mathrm{h} v-\phi$
So on increasing $v$, kinetic energy increases.

subject: Physics
q: Two sources of light emit with a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. The ratio of number of photons of visible light emitted by each source having wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> respectively, will be :
question: Two sources of light emit with a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. The ratio of number of photons of visible light emitted by each source having wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> respectively, will be :
solution:
Let n1 and n2 be the number of photons coming out from the first and the second source of light respectively per second.

So, for the first source,

n1×hcλ1=200   ...1

And, for the second source,

n2×hcλ2=200   ...2

From equations (1) and (2), it implies that

n1n2=λ1λ2=300500⇒n1n2=35

subject: Physics
q:
The temperature of an ideal gas in three dimensions is 300 K. The corresponding de-Broglie wavelength of the electron approximately at 300 K is:

me=mass of electron =9×10-31 kg, h=Planck constant =6.6×10-34 J s, kB=Boltzmann constant=1.38×10-23 J K-1
question:
The temperature of an ideal gas in three dimensions is 300 K. The corresponding de-Broglie wavelength of the electron approximately at 300 K is:

me=mass of electron =9×10-31 kg, h=Planck constant =6.6×10-34 J s, kB=Boltzmann constant=1.38×10-23 J K-1
solution:
k=1.38×10-23 J K-1

Mass of electron =9×10-31 kg

⇒λD=h2m×32×kT=h3mkT

⇒λD=6.6×10-343×9×10-31×1.38×10-23×300=6.6×10-34105.72×10-27=62 Aο

Thus x=62 Aο

subject: Physics
q: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle, a proton and an electron have the same kinetic energy. Which one of the following is correct in case of their de-Broglie wavelength:
question: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle, a proton and an electron have the same kinetic energy. Which one of the following is correct in case of their de-Broglie wavelength:
solution:
As we know, de-Broglie wavelength is given by,

λ=hp

and p=2mK

Therefore,

λ=h2mK

As kinetic energy is the same for all particles,

∴λ∝1m

∵mα>mp>me

λe>λp>λα

subject: Physics
q: When light of frequency twice the threshold frequency is incident on the metal plate, the maximum velocity of emitted electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When the frequency of incident radiation is increased to five times the threshold value, the maximum velocity of emitted electron becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
question: When light of frequency twice the threshold frequency is incident on the metal plate, the maximum velocity of emitted electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When the frequency of incident radiation is increased to five times the threshold value, the maximum velocity of emitted electron becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
solution:
Maximum kinetic energy is expressed as Kmax=hf-ϕ, here, ϕ is work function of metal.

For first case, 12mv12=h2f0−f0

For second case, 12mv22=h5f0−f0

⇒v22v12=41⇒v2v1=2

subject: Physics
q: An electron of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and a photon have same energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">.</mo></math> The ratio of wavelength of electron to that of photon is : (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> being the velocity of light)
question: An electron of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and a photon have same energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">.</mo></math> The ratio of wavelength of electron to that of photon is : (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> being the velocity of light)
solution:
λ1=h2mE

λ2=hcE

λ1λ2=1cE2m1/2

subject: Physics
q:
The stopping potential Vo (in volt) as a function of frequency (v) for a sodium emitter, is shown in the figure. The work function of sodium, form the data plotted in the figure, will be:
(Given: Planck’s constant h=6.63×10-34 J s , electron charge ( e ) =1.6×10-19 C)
question:
The stopping potential Vo (in volt) as a function of frequency (v) for a sodium emitter, is shown in the figure. The work function of sodium, form the data plotted in the figure, will be:
(Given: Planck’s constant h=6.63×10-34 J s , electron charge ( e ) =1.6×10-19 C)
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, hν=ϕ+eV0  .

⇒V0=hνe-ϕe  ......1

At ν=4×1014 Hz, V0=0.

Putting the value in equation 1, we get

 0=hve-ϕe

So, Work function (ϕ)=hνe  in eV
=6.63×10-34×4×10141.6×10-19
=1.66 eV

subject: Physics
q:
In photoelectric effect, the stopping potential $\left(\mathrm{V}_0\right) \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s}$ frequency $(\nu)$ curve is plotted.
( h is the Planck's constant and $\phi_0$ is work function of metal )
(A) $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ is linear.
(B) The slope of $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ curve $=\frac{\phi_0}{\mathrm{~h}}$
(C) h constant is related to the slope of $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ line.
(D) The value of electric charge of electron is not required to determine $h$ using the $V_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ curve.
(E) The work function can be estimated without knowing the value of $h$.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
In photoelectric effect, the stopping potential $\left(\mathrm{V}_0\right) \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s}$ frequency $(\nu)$ curve is plotted.
( h is the Planck's constant and $\phi_0$ is work function of metal )
(A) $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ is linear.
(B) The slope of $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ curve $=\frac{\phi_0}{\mathrm{~h}}$
(C) h constant is related to the slope of $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ line.
(D) The value of electric charge of electron is not required to determine $h$ using the $V_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \nu$ curve.
(E) The work function can be estimated without knowing the value of $h$.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{hv}=\phi+\mathrm{KE}_{\max } \\
& \mathrm{KE}_{\max }=\mathrm{eV}_0 \\
& \mathrm{~V}_0=\frac{\mathrm{hv}-\phi}{\mathrm{e}}
\end{aligned}$
(A) $\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s} \mathrm{V}$ is linear correct
(B) Slope
$\mathrm{v}_0=\left(\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{e}}\right) \mathrm{v}-\frac{\phi}{\mathrm{e}} \text { Wrong }$

Slope $\frac{h}{e}$
(C) Correct
(D) Incorrect
(E) Correct

subject: Physics
q:
This question has statement $1$ and statement $2$. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements
Statement 1 : Davisson - germer experiment established the wave nature of electrons.
Statement 2 : If electrons have wave nature, they can interfere and show diffraction.
question:
This question has statement $1$ and statement $2$. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements
Statement 1 : Davisson - germer experiment established the wave nature of electrons.
Statement 2 : If electrons have wave nature, they can interfere and show diffraction.
options: Statement $1$ is false, Statement $2$ is true
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is false
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is the correct explanation for statement $1$
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is true, Statement $2$ is not the correct explanation for statement $1$.
solution: Davisson - Germer experiment showed that electron beams can undergo diffraction when passed through atomic crystals. This shows the wave nature of electrons as waves can exhibit interference and diffraction.

subject: Physics
q: The kinetic energy of emitted electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> when the light incident on the metal has wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. To double the kinetic energy, the incident light must have wavelength:
question: The kinetic energy of emitted electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> when the light incident on the metal has wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. To double the kinetic energy, the incident light must have wavelength:
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation, kinetic energy of an electron is E when the incident wavelength is λ is E=hcλ-ϕ     ...1

When kinetic energy doubles, then, 2E=hcλ'-ϕ   ...2

Subtracting 2-1, we have 

E=hc1λ'-1λ

⇒λ'=hcλEλ+hc

subject: Physics
q: Average force exerted on a non-reflecting surface at normal incidence is $2.4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~N}$. If $360 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{cm}^2$ is the light energy flux during span of 1 hour 30 minutes, Then the area of the surface is:
question: Average force exerted on a non-reflecting surface at normal incidence is $2.4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~N}$. If $360 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{cm}^2$ is the light energy flux during span of 1 hour 30 minutes, Then the area of the surface is:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Pressure }=\frac{I}{C}=\frac{F}{A} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{360}{10^{-4} \times 3 \times 10^8}=\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-4}}{A} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}=2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^2=0.02 \mathrm{~m}^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: An electron with speed $v$ and a photon with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> have the same de-Broglie wavelength. If the kinetic energy and momentum of electron are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and that of photon are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msub></math> respectively. Which of the following is correct?
question: An electron with speed $v$ and a photon with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> have the same de-Broglie wavelength. If the kinetic energy and momentum of electron are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and that of photon are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msub></math> respectively. Which of the following is correct?
solution:
We have, λp=hpp and λe=hpe

Given here, λe=λp, then pp=pe

Ratio of their energy is EphEe=hcλPpe22m=2mcpe=2mcmv=2cv

subject: Physics
q: An electron accelerated through a potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> has a de-Broglie wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. When the potential is changed to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, its de-Broglie wavelength increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> is equal to :
question: An electron accelerated through a potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> has a de-Broglie wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. When the potential is changed to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, its de-Broglie wavelength increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> is equal to :
solution:
Let initial wavelength be λ, then after 50% increase wavelength will become 1.5λ.

Now, KE=P22m=eV & P=hλ

Therefore, eV=hλ22m.

So we can write

eV1=hλ22m   ...Eq(1) and 

eV2=h1.5λ22m   ...Eq(2)

From both equations, we get

V1V2=1.52=94

subject: Physics
q: Which figure shows the correct variation of applied potential difference (V) with photoelectric current (I) at two different intensities of light $\left(\mathrm{I}_1 < \mathrm{I}_2\right)$ of same wavelengths :
question: Which figure shows the correct variation of applied potential difference (V) with photoelectric current (I) at two different intensities of light $\left(\mathrm{I}_1 < \mathrm{I}_2\right)$ of same wavelengths :
solution:
Given lights are of same wavelength.
Hence stopping potential will remain same.
Since $I_2>I_1$, hence saturation current corresponding to $\mathrm{I}_2$ will be greater than that corresponding to $\mathrm{I}_1$.

subject: Physics
q: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron is the same as that of a photon. If velocity of electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the velocity of light, then the ratio of K.E. of electron and K.E. of photon will be:
question: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron is the same as that of a photon. If velocity of electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the velocity of light, then the ratio of K.E. of electron and K.E. of photon will be:
solution:
For photon,

EP=hcλp⇒λp=hcEP

For electron

λe=hmeve=hve2 Ke

Given ve=0.25c. So,

λe=h×0.25c2 Ke=hc8 Ke

Also λp=λe. Thus,

hcEp=hc8Ke⇒KeEp=18

subject: Physics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the maximum kinetic energies of photo-electrons emitted when two monochromatic beams of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, respectively are incident on a metallic surface. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> then :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the maximum kinetic energies of photo-electrons emitted when two monochromatic beams of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, respectively are incident on a metallic surface. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> then :
solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation

h⁡cλ=ϕ0+KEmax, where ϕ0 is a work function of a metal

∴ K⁡ 1 = h⁡ c λ 1 - ϕ 0 … i

        K⁡ 2 = h⁡ c λ 2 - ϕ 0 … i i

orK1-K2=hc1λ1-1λ2

                      =hc13λ2-1λ2=-2hc3λ2Givenλ1=3λ2

                      = - 2 3 K⁡ 2 + ϕ 0 Using (ii)

or⁡ K⁡ 1 = K⁡ 2 - 2 3 K⁡ 2 - 2 3 ϕ 0 = K⁡ 2 3 - 2 3 ϕ 0

or K⁡ 1 < K⁡ 2 3

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>31</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> travels in a medium with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and a photon of radiation of linear momentum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> travels in a vacuum. The wavelength of the photon is ____ times the wavelength of the particle.
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>31</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> travels in a medium with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and a photon of radiation of linear momentum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> travels in a vacuum. The wavelength of the photon is ____ times the wavelength of the particle.
solution:
For photon λ1=hP=6.6×10-3410-27
For particle λ2=hmv=6.6×10-349.1×10-31×106
∴ λ1λ2=910

q: Given below are two statements : Statement I : Figure shows the variation of stopping potential with frequency $(v)$ for the two photosensitive materials $\mathrm{M}_1$ and $\mathrm{M}_2$. The slope gives value of $\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{e}}$, where $\mathrm{h}$ is Planck's constant, $\mathrm{e}$ is the charge of electron. Statement II : $\mathrm{M}_2$ will emit photoelectrons of greater kinetic energy for the incident radiation having same frequency. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question: Given below are two statements : Statement I : Figure shows the variation of stopping potential with frequency $(v)$ for the two photosensitive materials $\mathrm{M}_1$ and $\mathrm{M}_2$. The slope gives value of $\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{e}}$, where $\mathrm{h}$ is Planck's constant, $\mathrm{e}$ is the charge of electron. Statement II : $\mathrm{M}_2$ will emit photoelectrons of greater kinetic energy for the incident radiation having same frequency. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect

subject: Physics
q: The threshold frequency of a metal with work function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>63</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> is :
question: The threshold frequency of a metal with work function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>63</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> is :
solution:
The formula to calculate the work function of the metal is given by

ϕ0=hv0   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

6.63×1.6×10-19=6.63×10-34v0⇒v0=1.6×10-1910-34⇒v0=1.6×1015 Hz

subject: Physics
q: The light of two different frequencies whose photons have energies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>eV</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>eV</mi></math> respectively, illuminate a metallic surface whose work function is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>eV</mi></math> successively. The ratio of maximum speeds of emitted electrons for the two frequencies respectively will be :
question: The light of two different frequencies whose photons have energies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>eV</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>eV</mi></math> respectively, illuminate a metallic surface whose work function is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>eV</mi></math> successively. The ratio of maximum speeds of emitted electrons for the two frequencies respectively will be :
solution:
Expression for maximum kinetic energy can be written as, KEmax=E-ϕ

KEmax, 1=E1-ϕ=3.8-0.6=3.2 eV, KEmax, 2=E2-ϕ=1.4-0.6=0.8 eV

KEmax, 1KEmax, 2=3.20.8=4⇒4=12 mv1212 mv22 

⇒v12v22=4 ⇒v1v2=2:1

subject: Physics
q: Light is incident on a metallic plate having work function $110 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{~J}$. If the produced photoelectrons have zero kinetic energy then the angular frequency of the incident light is $\_\_\_\_$ rad/s. $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}\right)$.
question: Light is incident on a metallic plate having work function $110 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{~J}$. If the produced photoelectrons have zero kinetic energy then the angular frequency of the incident light is $\_\_\_\_$ rad/s. $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}\right)$.
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, $h u = W + KE$.
With zero kinetic energy, $h u = W$.
The frequency is $ u = rac{W}{h} = rac{110 imes 10^{-20}}{6.63 imes 10^{-34}} = 16.59 imes 10^{14}$ Hz.
The angular frequency is $\omega = 2\pi u = 2\pi imes 16.59 imes 10^{14}$
$= 104.25 imes 10^{14} = 1.04 imes 10^{16}$ rad/s.

subject: Physics
q: When a metal surface is illuminated by light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>, the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. When the same surface is illuminated by light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>λ</mi></math>, stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The threshold wavelength for this surface is :
question: When a metal surface is illuminated by light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>, the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. When the same surface is illuminated by light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>λ</mi></math>, stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The threshold wavelength for this surface is :
solution:
From photoelectric effect equation, we can write E=ϕ+Kmax

and work function can be written as ϕ=hcλ0.

Also, maximum kinetic energy Kmax=eV0

For first case:

8e=hcλ-hcλ0   ... (i)

For second case:

2e=hc3λ-hcλ0    ... (ii)

After using equation (ii) in (i), we get

4hc3λ-hcλ0=hcλ-hcλ0

⇒λ0=9λ

subject: Physics
q: Electrons are accelerated through a potential difference $\mathrm{V}$ and protons are accelerated through a potential difference $4 \mathrm{~V}$. The de-Broglie wavelengths are $\lambda_e$ and $\lambda_p$ for electrons and protons respectively. The ratio of $\frac{\lambda_e}{\lambda_p}$ is given by: (given $m_e$ is mass of electron and $m_p$ is mass of proton).
question: Electrons are accelerated through a potential difference $\mathrm{V}$ and protons are accelerated through a potential difference $4 \mathrm{~V}$. The de-Broglie wavelengths are $\lambda_e$ and $\lambda_p$ for electrons and protons respectively. The ratio of $\frac{\lambda_e}{\lambda_p}$ is given by: (given $m_e$ is mass of electron and $m_p$ is mass of proton).
solution:
Energy in joule (E)
$=$ charge $\times$ potential diff. in volt $\mathrm{E}_{\text {electron }}=\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{V}$ and $\mathrm{E}_{\text {proton }}=\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}} 4 \mathrm{~V}$
de-Broglie wavelength $\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mE}}}$
$$
\begin{array}{r}
\lambda_{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{eV}}} \text { and } \lambda_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{e} 4 \mathrm{~V}}} \\
\left(\because \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{P}}\right)
\end{array}
$$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{e}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{P}}} & =\frac{\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{eV}}}}{\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{P}} \mathrm{e} 4 \mathrm{~V}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{P}} \mathrm{e} 4 \mathrm{~V}}{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{eV}}} \\ & =2 \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A particle is travelling <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times as fast as an electron. Assuming the ratio of de-Broglie wavelength of a particle to that of electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></math> the mass of the particle is :-
question: A particle is travelling <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times as fast as an electron. Assuming the ratio of de-Broglie wavelength of a particle to that of electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></math> the mass of the particle is :-
solution:
λ=hp
λpλe=pepp=mevempVp
2=mempve4ve
∴ mp=me8
